skill.name: analyze-project
skill.description: Read-only analysis skill for deep learning research repositories. It maps model structure, training and inference entrypoints, configuration relationships, likely insertion points, and suspicious implementation patterns without modifying project code.
skill.summary: Map deep learning repositories and review implementation risks without changing code.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high risk, but the flagged weak cryptography, eval, obfuscation, and shell-command findings are false positives from descriptions, Markdown backticks, or formatted output strings. The remaining concern is local filesystem scope: the helper resolves user-provided repository, context, and output paths, reads files recursively, and writes analysis artifacts without enforcing a workspace boundary.

low_findings.title: User-Supplied Filesystem Scope Is Not Restricted
low_findings.description: The script resolves caller-provided repository, context, and output paths, recursively reads repository files, and writes analysis artifacts. This is consistent with a local analysis helper, but marketplace users should run it only on intended project directories and output paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem reads and writes are directly visible in the script, and the paths come from command-line arguments. I found no evidence of exfiltration or destructive behavior, so the issue is scope control rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Prompt References Files Outside Skill Directory
low_findings.description: The skill documentation points to a relative parent-directory reference file. This appears to be shared documentation, but external references can reduce reviewability if the marketplace package does not include the target file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative parent path is explicit in the skill file. It is not a path traversal exploit by itself because it is documentation, but it should be reviewed for packaging clarity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Eval Finding Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner flagged eval at line 313, but the code checks for the text .eval() inside repository files to warn about model evaluation behavior. It does not call Python eval or execute dynamic code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code performs substring checks on file contents and appends a message. There is no dynamic code execution at the flagged location.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Shell Command Findings Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner flagged many backtick-like patterns, but the reviewed locations are Markdown formatting in skill text or formatted strings in generated reports. I found no subprocess, shell, popen, or os.system usage in the reviewed script.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged constructs are visible as Markdown code spans or strings being written to output files. A targeted search found no command execution API in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography in descriptive text and argparse usage. The reviewed files do not import cryptographic libraries, calculate hashes, encrypt data, or verify secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill metadata, a default prompt, and argparse setup. I found no evidence of cryptographic operations at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Obfuscation Heuristic Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner flagged multiple bracket chains near the script start, but the beginning of the file contains normal Python imports and constant dictionaries. No evidence of JSFuck-style obfuscation was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited area is readable Python source with ordinary imports and regex configuration. The heuristic likely matched bracket-heavy dictionary syntax elsewhere, not obfuscated execution.

content.value_statement: Deep learning repositories can be hard to understand before making changes. This skill creates a conservative map of entrypoints, configs, modules, and suspicious patterns.
actual_capabilities: Maps likely training, inference, evaluation, model, and config entrypoints.
Ranks task-relevant files using repository paths and optional analysis context.
Extracts Python symbols such as classes, functions, constructors, and forward methods.
Builds Markdown summaries for research maps, change maps, risk notes, and evaluation contracts.
Flags suspicious implementation patterns as heuristics for human review.
Supports JSON stdout mode for integration with other local tooling.
limitations: It does not prove that a suspicious pattern is a confirmed bug.
It relies on filenames, text signals, and Python syntax parsing, not full program execution.
It can read many files under the selected repository path.
It writes analysis artifacts to the selected output directory unless JSON mode is used.

use_cases.title: Onboard to a Research Repository
use_cases.description: Create a first map of model files, configs, and runnable entrypoints before editing code.

use_cases.title: Review a Paper Implementation
use_cases.description: Identify training, evaluation, and metric paths that need human verification before reproduction work.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Conservative Change Plan
use_cases.description: Find likely insertion points and risky patterns before proposing architecture or config changes.

prompt_templates.title: Map This Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this deep learning repository. Summarize the training, inference, evaluation, model, and config entrypoints without modifying files.

prompt_templates.title: Find Relevant Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this repository for a segmentation task. Identify task-relevant files, data interfaces, metrics, and likely change points.

prompt_templates.title: Review Risky Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the project for suspicious implementation patterns. Mark each item as a heuristic and explain what evidence would confirm it.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Evaluation Contract
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the repository using my evaluation command and dataset context. Produce an evaluation contract with expected artifacts and comparison cautions.

output_examples.input: Analyze a vision model repository before adding a new adapter.
output: A concise project summary with likely train, inference, evaluation, model, and config entrypoints.
A change map listing candidate module files and data interface files for review.
A risk note that separates suspicious heuristics from confirmed defects.

output_examples.input: Prepare a reproduction audit for a benchmark implementation.
output: An evaluation contract with dataset, benchmark, primary metric, metric goal, and expected artifacts.
A research map that links config files, metric files, and evaluation entrypoints.
A status summary suitable for tracking analysis outputs.

best_practices: Run the helper only on the intended repository path.
Provide dataset, benchmark, and evaluation command context when available.
Treat suspicious pattern output as a review queue, not as final proof.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to run training jobs or heavy evaluations by default.
Do not let it replace a security review of untrusted repositories.
Do not accept insertion-point suggestions without reading the referenced files.

faq.question: Does this skill modify project code?
faq.answer: No. The skill is designed for read-only repository analysis and writes only analysis artifacts.

faq.question: Can it run training or evaluation commands?
faq.answer: No. It maps entrypoints and evaluation contracts, but it does not execute heavy jobs by design.

faq.question: What repositories work best?
faq.answer: It is best suited for deep learning research repositories with Python files, configs, and clear training or evaluation scripts.

faq.question: Does it confirm bugs automatically?
faq.answer: No. It labels suspicious patterns as heuristics that need human verification.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing it?
faq.answer: Review the local filesystem path scope and confirm that referenced shared documentation is included or documented.
